unit.name: Syntactic ambiguity and why parses matter
unit.description: Shows how attachment and coordination ambiguities create multiple readings, and motivates syntax as a practical tool for interpretation and information extraction.
unit.summary: This segment motivates parsing through ambiguity. Using headline examples such as Scientists count whales from space, the lecture shows that a prepositional phrase can attach either to the verb event or to a nearby noun, yielding different meanings [ts=929s] [ts=978s]. The instructor emphasizes that English prepositional phrases frequently create globally ambiguous structures, and with multiple successive PPs the number of readings grows quickly according to Catalan-style noncrossing choices, reaching 13 readings for four PPs in the example sentence about an acquisition [ts=1165s] [ts=1311s]. He then broadens the discussion to coordination scope, apposition, and infinitival attachment with additional newspaper headlines, stressing that different languages realize ambiguity differently rather than avoiding it entirely [ts=1355s] [ts=1621s]. The segment ends with a practical NLP use case: dependency patterns can be used to extract structured facts such as protein-protein interactions from biomedical text by locating verbs like interacts and their arguments and coordinated entities [ts=1635s] [ts=1718s].

key_points.text: Prepositional phrase attachment can change meaning, as in Scientists count whales from space.
Multiple PP attachments create many possible readings, with counts growing combinatorially under noncrossing constraints.
Coordination scope ambiguity can make a phrase describe one entity or multiple entities.
Dependency patterns support information extraction by identifying verbs and their syntactic arguments, including coordinated arguments.

kp_catalog.name: Syntactic ambiguity and attachment
kp_catalog.description: The fact that one surface word sequence can support multiple syntactic analyses, especially through PP attachment, coordination scope, and modifier scope.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because syntactic ambiguity and attachment clarifies the fact that one surface word sequence can support multiple syntactic analyses, especially through PP attachment, coordination scope, and modifier scope, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Dependency grammar
kp_catalog.description: A syntactic representation built from head-dependent relations, where each word is linked to the word it modifies or depends on.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because dependency grammar formalizes how a syntactic representation built from head-dependent relations, where each word is linked to the word it modifies or depends on, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
